figures.gloss: The sacramental ayahuasca brew used in the Santo Daime church, prepared ceremonially from the Jagube vine and Rainha leaf. It is treated not as a symbol of the divine, but as a divine teacher and the literal blood of Christ.
figures.facet: The Daime possesses its own agency. It actively directs the ritual, administers spiritual healings, and delivers 'peias' (purgative, somatic disciplines) from the inside out.
figures.provenance: Institutionalized by Mestre Irineu in 1930s Brazil, synthesizing Amazonian vegetalismo with folk Catholicism.

figures.gloss: A Brazilian rubber tapper who, under the influence of ayahuasca, received the 'Santo Daime' doctrine from a spiritual apparition he identified as the Virgin Mary, known as the Queen of the Forest.
figures.facet: He grounded the chaotic, individualistic shamanic ayahuasca trip into a structured, communal, uniformed, and heavily Christianized religious framework to protect his followers.

figures.gloss: The esoteric name for the Christic spirit that dwells within the Daime brew and the spiritual lineage of Mestre Irineu. A portmanteau often translated as 'God and his soldiers'.
figures.facet: Christ is decoupled from historical Judea; salvation and revelation are relocated into the Amazonian flora and accessed via direct, visceral ingestion rather than scripture.
figures.provenance: Central theological concept in the Santo Daime hinários.

figures.gloss: Collections of hymns 'received' from the astral plane by elders of the church. Sung continuously during all-night ceremonies while the congregation dances in strict geometric formations.
figures.facet: Santo Daime has virtually no written theology or sermons. The doctrine is entirely encoded in rhythm and song, which serves as an auditory navigational map for the collective psychedelic experience.
figures.provenance: Core ritual texts of Santo Daime, originating with Mestre Irineu's 'O Cruzeiro'.

figures.gloss: The sacrament of the União do Vegetal (UDV), made from the Mariri vine (representing 'Force') and the Chacruna leaf (representing 'Light'). It is used to achieve mental concentration and moral evolution.
figures.facet: Unlike the ecstatic, rhythmic ceremonies of Daime, UDV sessions emphasize stillness, intense mental clarity, and verbal inquiry to integrate the visions into everyday moral uprightness.
figures.provenance: Central sacrament of the UDV, formalized by Mestre Gabriel in 1961.

figures.gloss: The Marian apparition — identified in Santo Daime doctrine with the Virgin Mary — who is held to have appeared to Raimundo Irineu Serra in the Amazon in the 1920s-30s and commissioned him to found the Daime doctrine; she is addressed directly in many hinário hymns and understood to preside continuously over the works.
figures.facet: Catholic Marian devotion is transposed onto the rainforest itself: sovereignty is invested not in a walled shrine but in the living jungle, making the doctrine's female principle continuous with, rather than separate from, the landscape that produces the sacrament.
figures.provenance: Santo Daime oral tradition and hinário lyrics addressing her as 'Rainha da Floresta'/'Rainha do Mar'; Edward MacRae, Guiado pela Lua (1992); Alberto Groisman's ethnography of Santo Daime cosmology.

figures.gloss: José Gabriel da Costa (1922-1971), the Bahian rubber tapper who in 1961 founded the União do Vegetal (UDV) in Porto Velho, Rondônia, reframing the ayahuasca sacrament (hoasca) as an instrument of mental concentration and progressive moral evolution within a graded, initiatory church hierarchy.
figures.facet: Where Irineu built Santo Daime's doctrine around ecstatic collective dance and hymn, Mestre Gabriel structured the UDV around seated stillness, verbal catechism, and ranked degrees of membership — discipline, not motion, as the technology of the vision.
figures.provenance: Sandra Goulart's historical scholarship on the UDV's founding; União do Vegetal's own institutional accounts of Mestre Gabriel.

figures.gloss: Sebastião Mota de Melo (1920-1990), a successor-claimant within Mestre Irineu's Santo Daime lineage who broke from the original Alto Santo community in the 1970s to found what became CEFLURIS, relocating the church in 1983 to the forest community of Céu do Mapiá in Amazonas and opening the doctrine to lay practitioners abroad.
figures.facet: Where Irineu founded the doctrine and Alto Santo preserved it in place, Padrinho Sebastião is the branch through which Santo Daime became exportable — the line that actually carries the Amazonian sacrament into churches on multiple continents.
figures.provenance: Alex Polari de Alverga's insider account of CEFLURIS; Edward MacRae's scholarship on the Santo Daime schism and the founding of Céu do Mapiá.

figures.gloss: The days-long ritual preparation of the Daime: men pounding the jagube (mariri) vine in a strict gendered division of labor, women selecting and washing the rainha (chacrona) leaves, the two cooked together over an open fire in a fixed liturgical order and accompanied by hymn, from which the finished sacrament emerges.
figures.facet: The brew's sanctity is understood to begin at the cookfire rather than the altar: agency is invested in the labor of making, so the Feitio functions as much as the sacrament's origin-rite as the drinking of the Daime itself.
figures.provenance: Edward MacRae's ethnography of Santo Daime ritual practice; Alberto Groisman's fieldwork on the feitio's gendered division of labor.

figures.gloss: The 'Little Boat' — the ayahuasca church founded in 1945 by Daniel Pereira de Mattos (Mestre Daniel) in Rio Branco, Acre — the third of the Brazilian ayahuasca religions alongside Santo Daime and the UDV, distinguished by an explicitly maritime liturgical idiom (the chapel as a ship, its leader as 'captain') and heavy incorporation of Kardecist mediumship and Afro-Brazilian spirit incorporation alongside the sacrament.
figures.facet: Where Santo Daime encodes doctrine in march-formation and hymn and the UDV in seated catechism, Barquinha stages the ritual as a voyage — mediums receive spirits of Kardecist and Afro-Brazilian provenance aboard a chapel furnished like a ship's deck.
figures.provenance: Alberto Groisman and Sandra Goulart's comparative scholarship on the three Brazilian ayahuasca religions; Clodomir Monteiro da Silva's ethnography of the Barquinha.

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: Mestre Irineu is considered the human vessel or conduit for the Christic spirit of Juramidam.
Intra-tradition bond: The Daime provides the spiritual force, while the hinarios provide the structural container and map for the visions.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Santo Daime doctrine explicitly identifies Juramidam as the return of Jesus Christ, incarnated in the Amazonian brew.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are consumed physical elements that act as the active, living mediator between the human and divine realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Hoasca is fundamentally conceptualized as the union of masculine force (the vine) and feminine light/receptivity (the leaf).
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions encode the geography of the spiritual world and ancestral law entirely within unbroken, transmitted song.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are heroic figures who retrieve a potent, divine technology from the spiritual realm and institutionalize it for humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a rigid, divinely revealed set of operating instructions meant to maintain the cosmic order through exact ritual compliance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the innate, luminous fragment of the divine that is awakened or liberated from within the physical form.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The UDV theology of the vine and leaf parallels the Vedic dualism of primal force/nature and pure consciousness/light.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an invisible, transmissible spiritual energy that can be harnessed, gathered, and directed through ritual action.
Intra-tradition bond: the Queen of the Forest is held to have commissioned Mestre Irineu with the Santo Daime doctrine in a series of forest visions.
Santo Daime doctrine explicitly identifies the Queen of the Forest as the Virgin Mary — a Marian apparition relocated into the Amazon canopy.
Intra-tradition bond: Mestre Gabriel formalized hoasca as the UDV's central sacrament in 1961.
The founder-figures of the UDV and Santo Daime respectively, arising independently within the same early-20th-century Amazonian rubber-tapper milieu of vegetalismo.
Intra-tradition bond: Padrinho Sebastião is the successor-claimant who carried Mestre Irineu's lineage from Alto Santo into the international CEFLURIS/ICEFLU expansion.
Intra-tradition bond: the Feitio is the rite of making that produces the Daime; the brew's later agency is understood to originate in this ordered ritual labor.
Barquinha, Santo Daime, and the UDV are the three sister ayahuasca religions that emerged from the same early-20th-century Acre/Rondônia rubber-tapper milieu.
Barquinha's mediums receive spirits under Kardecist protocol imported directly into the ayahuasca liturgy — the same mesa blanca session-grammar redeployed aboard the 'ship'.

The same ayahuasca brew re-theologized: Amazonian vegetalismo's plant-teacher revealing icaros and kené versus Mestre Irineu's 1930s Santo Daime synthesis, which reframes the identical brew as the literal blood of Christ.

dossier.essence: A modern comparative grouping of two Brazilian churches built around a psychoactive sacrament taken as the literal vehicle of the divine: Santo Daime (founded by Raimundo Irineu Serra, 'Mestre Irineu', in 1930s Acre) and the Uniao do Vegetal (UDV, founded by Jose Gabriel da Costa, 'Mestre Gabriel', in 1961). Both draw their brew from the same ayahuasca vine and DMT-bearing leaf, under different names and theologies, and treat it not as a symbol but as an agent that teaches, heals, and reveals directly. (Bwiti, the Gabonese iboga religion once catalogued alongside them under this umbrella, shares the same plant-as-teacher logic but is unrelated in lineage and now has its own dossier.)
dossier.cosmos: The two churches fuse Amazonian plant-shamanism (vegetalismo) with folk Catholicism and, in Santo Daime, Kardecist spiritism: Christ and the divine are met within the brew, and the world is a moral order navigated through the vision (miracao). The spirit world is not merely believed in but directly and repeatedly visited in life through the sacrament.
dossier.practice: Santo Daime holds all-night 'works' of hymn-singing (hinos) and synchronized dancing in uniformed ranks; the UDV holds seated, quieter sessions of mental concentration and ethical teaching led by a Mestre. Both demand discipline, community, and a tightly bounded ritual container for an intense pharmacological experience.
dossier.canon: Largely oral and musical rather than scriptural: Santo Daime's authority lives in its received hymnals (hinarios, beginning with Irineu's 'O Cruzeiro'), while the UDV transmits Mestre Gabriel's oral teachings through its Mestres.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds the chemically induced encounter as the ground of religious authority — revelation sought not in text or inherited tradition but in a repeatable, bodily ingested experience of the sacred.
sources: Andrew Dawson, 'Santo Daime: A New World Religion' (2013)
Beatriz Caiuby Labate and Henrik Jungaberle (eds.), 'The Internationalization of Ayahuasca' (2011)
